questions.questionText: According to WHO, the four main behavioral risk factors responsible for most NCDs are:
questions.optionA: Poor hygiene, overcrowding, contaminated water, malnutrition
questions.optionB: Tobacco use, unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, and harmful alcohol use (these lead to 4 metabolic risk factors: HTN, obesity, hyperglycemia, hyperlipidemia)
questions.optionC: Genetic predisposition, age, sex, family history
questions.optionD: Air pollution, water pollution, noise pollution, radiation
questions.explanation: Core concept: 4 behavioral risk factors → 4 metabolic risk factors → 4 major NCDs. Why correct: WHO framework: behavioral (tobacco, diet, inactivity, alcohol) → metabolic (raised BP, overweight/obesity, raised blood sugar, raised blood lipids) → NCDs (CVD, cancer, chronic respiratory diseases, diabetes). These 4 NCDs cause 80% of premature NCD deaths. Why A wrong: These are risk factors for communicable diseases. Why C wrong: These are non-modifiable risk factors (important but not the 4 main behavioral ones). Why D wrong: Environmental risk factors but not the WHO's primary 4 behavioral. Clinical relevance: WHO '25 by 25' target: 25% relative reduction in premature NCD mortality by 2025 through addressing these 4 risk factors. Exam trap: 4-4-4 framework is a HIGH-YIELD table-based question topic. Memory aid: 'T-D-I-A → H-O-S-L → C-C-C-D (Tobacco-Diet-Inactivity-Alcohol → HTN-Obesity-Sugar-Lipids → CVD-Cancer-COPD-Diabetes).' Reference: Park – NCDs.
questions.conceptTag: NCD 4 behavioral risk factors WHO framework

questions.questionText: Population-based cancer screening is recommended for which of the following cancers in India?
questions.optionB: Cervical, breast, and oral cancer (under population-based screening program of India — VIA for cervical, CBE for breast, visual oral exam for oral)
questions.explanation: Core concept: India's NCD screening includes cervical + breast + oral cancer screening. Why correct: Government of India under NPCDCS: Cervical cancer: VIA (Visual Inspection with Acetic acid) for women 30-65 years. Breast cancer: CBE (Clinical Breast Examination) for women ≥30 years. Oral cancer: visual oral examination for all ≥30 years (especially tobacco users). Why A wrong: Lung cancer screening (low-dose CT for heavy smokers) — not population-based in India. Why C wrong: Prostate cancer (PSA) — not recommended for mass screening (overdiagnosis). Why D wrong: No screening tests available for brain/bone cancer. Clinical relevance: HWCs (Health and Wellness Centres) under Ayushman Bharat provide NCD screening at primary level. Exam trap: VIA (not Pap smear) is India's recommended cervical screening method at population level — low cost, immediate result. Memory aid: 'India screens: Cervix (VIA), Breast (CBE), Oral (Visual) — the 3 accessible cancers.' Reference: Park – NCD Screening.
questions.conceptTag: Cancer screening India VIA CBE oral cervical breast
